text:
Person1: Is this what you wanted to speak to me about?
Person2: In a way -- this girl, Thea. You must stay away from her.
Person1: I had a notion you had become selfappointed chaperone lately  why?
Person2: You are my friend.
Person1: And I'm your friend  but that doesn't explain why you are always trying to come between Thea and me?
Person2: If I told you  you wouldn't believe me -- but this much I can tell you  the girl is dangerous to you. Take a friend's advice -- an old man' s advice -- leave her alone -
Person1: That's ridiculous -- Thea's lovely, gentle -
Person2: Listen to what I say --
Person1: When you make sense I'll listen.

Person1: Captain, I've been observing the nurses on your shift. But naturally your own opinion is more informed than mine.
Person2: I'm glad you feel that way, Major, because you see it's a team effort... doctors, nurses, enlisted men... and I feel responsible for my whole team, and I want you to know I'm satisfied with them.
Person1: All of them?
Person2: That's right. We work well together.
Person1: Major Burns is far from satisfied.
Person2: That don't surprise me. If you're a good observer, you must have observed by now that Frank Burns is a jerk.
Person1: On the contrary, I've observed he's not only a good technical surgeon, he's a good military surgeon. And that includes how a man dresses and how he bears himself and his sense of what it means to be an officer in the United States Army.
Person2: And his track record, that don't count? Look, honey, when you watch the two shifts try to notice which one does the most work with the least fuss.
Person1: I've noticed that both nurses and enlisted men address you as 'Hawkeye.'
Person2: It's my name. Maybe that sounds silly to you but...
Person1: That kind of familiarity is inconsistent with maximum efficiency in a military organization.
Person2: Okay, Major, honey.  I'm going to have a couple shots scotch and go to bed. I'd normally ask you to join me but obviously you're a female version of the routine Regular Army clown. And that turns me off, so just leave my outfit alone and we'll get along fine.  See you around the campus.
Person1: I wonder how a degenerated person like you could have reached a position of responsibility in the Army Medical Corps.
Person2: Sister, if I knew the answer to that I sure as hell wouldn't be here.

Person1: No, no, no, that's crazy, you're out of your mind, he's absolutely out of his mind - -
Person2: Wait a minute.  What exactly does this mean?

Person1: An evil vet?
Person2: No. Maybe, like, work in a petting zoo or something.
Person1: An evil petting zoo?
Person2: You always do that!  Anyways, this is really hard, because, you know, my Dad is really evil.

Person1: I hear you've got visitors.
Person2: Would you guys...
Person1: No time for any of that crap any more, lady... I'll rip your lungs out if you don't answer fast.

Person1: It's an outcall service run out of an apartment.  I don't meet the other girls.  Aren't you gonna get undressed?
Person2: Where is escort service?
Person1: That's confidential.  Could you put the money on the dresser?
Person2: I like to talk to the person who runs the service.  Can you give me address?
Person1: Look.  Do we have a problem here? There's no reason to have a problem. I'm gonna make you feel real good.  You wanna Czech girl? After I'm done with you, you won't miss her.  Now why don't you pay me?

Person1: No, hang on, I've not gone daft. You see, when I was little me mother let me make some treacle toffee one time in our back scullery. When I'd done she said to me, "Go and give some to the other kids." So, I said I would but I thought to meself, "She must think I'm soft." Anyroad, I was eating away there but I wanted somebody else to know how good it was so in the end I wound up giving it all away ... but I didn't mind, mind, 'cos I'd made the stuff in the first place. Well ... that's why you need other people... an audience ... to taste your treacle toffee, like. Eh ... does that sound as thickheaded to you as it does to me?
Person2: Not really but I'm probably not a toffee maker. How would you do those lines of mine?
Person1: Well, look at it this way, I mean, when you come right down to it, that girl, she's a bit of a scrubber, isn't she?
Person2: Is she?
Person1: Of course ... Look, if she was a Liverpool scrubber ...  Eh, fella, you want to try pulling the other one, it's got a full set of bells hanging off it ... Y'what? ... I know your sort, two cokes and a packet of cheese and onion crisps and suddenly it's love and we're stopping in an empty shop doorway. You're just after me body and y'can't have it ... so there!!
Person2: And you honestly think that's what she meant?
Person1: Oh, definitely, it sticks out a mile, she's trying to get him to marry her but he doesn't want ... well ... I don't reckon any fella's ever wanted to get married. But girls are like that, clever and cunning. You've got to laugh.

Person1: I swear to you, Argon, if you don't stop the meltdown that nanobot will be the last one I ever build.
Person2: Susan, I sense you are having difficulty understanding the situation you are presently in.  I ask that you keep in mind that I am ready to reduce an entire city to gelatin to get what I want.

Person1: Uh...wonderful. I know I'm strange, but in my own way, I love you
Person2: I love you too.

Person1: Lasher touched you and you loved it.
Person2: It wes a dream...!
Person1: Just like your mother - and all the others that felt him - and used his power - and went to hell with a smile!
Person2: Stop it!
Person1: Witches. Rowan.  that is what we are.
Person2: ENOUGH!!!

Person1: Wait -- what're you doing?
Person2: I'm going down.
Person1: Down? Down there?! Are you crazy!
Person2: I'm not leaving without those stones.
Person1: You're gonna get killed chasing after your damn fortune and glory!
Person2: Maybe... someday.  Not today.

Person1: We don't have a band! Y'all just shut up! We don't need no practicin' or managers cause we ain't no band! Morris ain't no genius and the rest of you are just losers. Am I the only one sane human bein' around here? Just get the hell out of my house and don't come back!
Person2: It's not your house, Doyle, it's Linda's.
Person1: I'll kill you, you faggot! You mind your own business. Now get out! Now, before I get too mad to turn back.

Person1: - All's I know is, ya got a problem, ya call a professional!
Person2: No! They said no cops! They were darned clear on that, Wade! They said you call the cops and we -
Person1: Well, a course they're gonna say that! But where's my protection? They got Jean here! I give these sons a bitches a million dollars, where's my guarantee they're gonna let her go.
Person2: Well, they -
Person1: A million dollars is a lot a damn money! And there they are, they got my daughter!
Person2: Yah, but think this thing through here, Wade. Ya give 'em what they want, why wont' they let her go? You gotta listen to me on this one, Wade.
Person1: Heck, you don't know! You're just whistlin' Dixie here! I'm sayin', the cops, they can advise us on this! I'm sayin' call a professional!
Person2: No! No cops! That's final! This is my deal here, Wade! Jean is my wife here!

Person1: Sorry to take up your time like that Trevor. Don't work too hard. Oh, before I forget. I talked your neighbor out of pressing charges.
Person2: What?
Person1: The whackjob in the black lipstick who lives down the hall? She wanted you arrested for harassment. I told her to chill out and smoke a joint. I'd look the other way as long as she did you know?
Person2: Thanks.
Person1: We're all here for you Trevor.

Person1: Steve...what you tried to do today...you tried to take me up on a high mountain...I couldn't go all the way...I failed you...  Will you forgive me?  Have I lost you, Steve?  Have I...?
Person2: Well, maybe I was wrong, too... But there's no doubt, Susie, that we have to face some serious things...
Person1: No one's ever stood up to my brother the way you did.
Person2: But you didn't do much about it, Susie.  You walked out, and there I was...solo...and not too good at that.
Person1: I just didn't think that I could antagonize him, Steve -- for OUR sake, I mean.
Person2: Susie, I was there for OUR sake, too.  But what a world it would be if we were all afraid to learn to walk and talk because it might offend poppa!  By the way, I think your brother was completely responsible for the smears...

Person1: Patrick Bateman's office.
Person2: Jean? Hello? Jean?
Person1: Patrick? Is that you?
Person2: Hello? Jean, I need help!
Person1: Where are you?
Person2: Jean-I'm not-
Person1: Craig McDermott called. He wants to meet you and David Van Patten and Tim Price at Harry's for drinks.
Person2: Oh God, what did you say, you dumb bitch?
Person1: Patrick? I can't hear you.
Person2: What are I doing?
Person1: Where are you? Patrick, what's wrong?
Person2: I don't think I'm gonna make it, Jean.

Person1: Ellie...God doesn't do things like that. I know you loved y'brother, but--
Person2: He can if He wants to. He can do anything, just like Inspector Gadget on TV. But I have to keep things ready for him, that's what I think. I've got his picture and I'm going to sit in his chair--

Person1: What, again?
Person2: Yes--you know what I've been thinking, Sally?--
Person1: What?--
Person2: I think we'll live in Europe after we're married?
Person1: I didn't know we were going to be married.
Person2: You didn't?--I guess I forgot to tell you--
Person1: You're a little ahead of yourself--the usual routine would be for you to propose, and if I accept, then you'd give me an engagement ring--
Person2: I'll give you plenty of rings as soon as I get the stones set--what do you think?
Person1: I think we ought to walk.

Person1: Rick, I want to talk to you.
Person2: Ah, Cole.  I don't remember ordering an asshole from room service.

Person1: They had it at the store, Paul, there was a whole batch of them there. As soon as I saw it, I slammed my money down. I got the first copy.
Person2: Then the roads are open...
Person1: The one to town is, but that's about it. I called the hospital and talked to the head orthopedic surgeon. I told him who you were and what had happened. He said as long as there's no infection, you're not in any danger, and as soon as the road to the hospital is open, they'll send an ambulance for you.
Person2: The phones are working?
Person1: Well, mine's still out. But the ones in town were working just fine. I called that agent of yours.  Oh, Paul, I peeked at the very beginning.  What a wonderful first page--just to read the name Misery Chastain...
Person2: My daughter must be going nuts.
Person1: ...it's like a visit from my oldest, dearest friend.
Person2: I was supposed to be home for her birthday three days ago.
Person1: Your agent said she would tell her you were okay. But I'm afraid you'll have to wait until tomorrow if you want to speak to her yourself.

Person1: ... Sid, be reasonable - I've been everywhere, there's no place left to check!
Person2: Try again.
Person1: Sure, try again - where? There's nothing, it's like he never existed!
Person2: Try again.
Person1: Sid, it's useless!
Person2: I said - try again.

Person1: Claudia, let's get married?
Person2: What! Get married?
Person1: Yes. We'll get married. You and I. What do you say?
Person2: What do I say? What can I say? No. At least, not yet. I don't know... I can't even think of it... at a time like this... Oh, but why did you have to ask me?
Person1: You look at me as though I had said something foolish...
Person2: And are you sure you want to marry me? Are you really sure...that you want to marry... me?
Person1: That's why I asked you...
Person2: So... Oh, how I wish that everything were so much simpler... that people could just come together by the color of their hair or the size of their shoes. What size shoe do you wear? Size 9. That's a very lovely size. But I'm sorry, I wear size 8.

Person1: What's happening to you? What happened to your face?
Person2: I don't know.
Person1: What do you mean?... You've been acting strange lately... Like the other night.
Person2: What night?
Person1: Last time I saw you.
Person2: I don't remember... What happened that night?
Person1: You sure weren't acting like the Pete Dayton I've always known.
Person2: Whatiya mean?
Person1: You were acting like a different person.

Person1: Major Dallas, if our calculations are correct you still have 57 hours owed to the Federal Army on your enlistment which is more than you will need for a mission of the utmost importance.
Person2: What mission?
Person1: To save the world.
Person2: Where have I heard this song before?
Person1: You're to leave immediately for Fhloston Paradise. Retrieve four Stones from the Diva Plavalaguna. And bring them back with the utmost discretion as possible. Any questions'?
Person2: Just one... why me?
Person1: Three reasons... One

Person1: You don't do that, you don't call him, you don't know to get involved in the bussiness of his, of his of my family. this is the family, me and him do you understand?  You understand? NO ONE ELSE. THERE IS NO ONE ELSE.  That man, his son does not exist.  HE IS DEAD.  HE IS DEAD and WHO TOLD YOU TO DO THAT?
Person2: Earl asked me, Linda, please, Linda, I'm sorry -- Earl asked me --
Person1: BULLSHIT. BULLSHIT HE DIDN'T ASK YOU, HE DOESN'T WANT HIM, HE DOESN'T WANT TO TALK TO HIM, SO FORGET YOU THAT HE ASKED THAT. THERE IS NO ONE BUT ME AND HIM.

Person1: Is he home right now?
Person2: Yeah.
Person1: Well, I think I can help a little more if I talk to him directly.
Person2: I don't know --
Person1: I'm sure he won't be angry once he realizes how concerned you are about him.
Person2: Okay, but if I get yelled at, I'm never gonna listen to this show again.
Person1: Fair enough.

Person1: What did you do then?
Person2: Shit, what difference does it make... Nobody's gonna believe me anyway...
Person1: Suzie!  Did this man rape you?
Person2: Okay!  He did.  He pushed me to the floor and he did it to me.  Now, can I go home?

Person1: Good morning.
Person2: Good morning.
Person1: Did you see what the First Bank stock opened at ? Very impressive.
Person2: Actually it's down .2 from the last quarters highest, but I think it will pick up.
Person1: I am equally impressed with your knowledge of the market. How long have you been with the company now ?
Person2: Four years. I came over from the Capitol merger in Chicago six months ago.
Person1: Were you involved in the incident where that guy killed himself in his office ?  I am sorry. Perhaps we should have dinner tonight to.... discuss your future.

Person1: Mom?
Person2: I've been so worried, dear. where are you?
Person1: Nashville.
Person2: Oh... Well.
Person1: The car broke down.
Person2: Oh, well, I knew that before you- even left. I told you, 'Kenny, that old Nash won't make it down the road.'  But you had to go.
Person1: Well, I got to Nashville. That's pretty far.
Person2: Next you'll be sick.
Person1: I feel really good.
Person2: How are you going to pay when you get sick?
Person1: I'm in a rooming house with Mr. Green.
Person2: Green?  Not Owen Green. Owen Green embezzled money from your grandfather
Person1: I don't know his first name.
Person2: Bet the sheets are grey.
Person1: No. No, they're white. He's got a nice dog.
Person2: Worst time at year for dogs is when they get fungus.
Person1: Fungus?
Person2: He doesn1t have fungus, does he?
Person1: I don't think so.
Person2: Well, you'll get it, skin as sensitive as yours.
Person1: How do you know if they do?

Person1: Tell them I'm keeping what's left. I earned it.  I probably killed some poor bastard for it.
Person2: He seems to think he's entitled to it all.
Person1: Not all.  They already have a third.  The question is how much of that are they willing to give to get you back?
Person2: You won't shoot me.  That's not your style.
Person1: We might hit a bump and the gun might go off.  Ever see that Tarantino movie -- where Travolta blew that guy away in the back seat -- purely by accident?
Person2: Do you have to point that?
Person1: Absolutely.  And Topanga Canyon has a hell of a lot of potholes if I recall.
Person2: He seems to be headed for Topanga.
Person1: I'm not trying to lose them.  Nor am I exceeding any speed limits.
Person2: The one thing you don't want is to attract the police.
Person1: Granted, the cops are not an alternative.  Certainly not with one of their own still locked in my trunk.
Person2: I don't hear him moving around anymore.
Person1: Those shots your associates got off may not have done him too much good.  That's on their head.  All I did I was put him there.
Person2: A typical mess-up.
Person1: What's that supposed to me?
Person2: I knew you were a loser the first night I laid eyes on you.
Person1: You, I would've noticed.
Person2: Oh no, you were too busy trying to keep some Croatian from slapping the shit out of his girlfriend. She showed her gratitude by almost taking your eye out with her spiked heel.
Person1: You were at the Emerald that night?
Person2: Naturally you didn't learn your lesson.
Person1: I guess I ought to stop seeing woman as victims.
Person2: I think it was my tone of voice more than anything else that sold you.  And when you thought I was being kicked around, I wish I could've seen your face.
Person1: You're enjoying this too much.

Person1: It's some kind of trick... a preliminary airstrike in response to our troop movement.
Person2: They are in our airspace. We would be within our rights.
Person1: The world would not look on us kindly if we shot down a civilian airliner.

Person1: They're like beasts. Wild.
Person2: Maybe they're angry because they're grown in meccano-breeders.
Person1: Instead of what? Nine months inside a woman. We're all raised the same but most of us don't become cubs in Cathedral.
Person2: Some people say children need human mothering.
Person1: Insane. Nurseries are better than any mother could be.
Person2: I'm only telling you what I've heard...  Haven't you ever wondered what your seed-mother was Like...?
Person1: Uh-uh.
Person2: I have.
Person1: When did you begin to question Lastday?
Person2: I don't remember exactly... except I was a Green.  What would you like to relive, Logan?
Person1: Let's see -- how long has it been?

Person1: Rose, Rose, Rose, you poor miserable little child, don't you know I love you? Do I have to put my hand on your body or kiss your pretty lips to prove it? You are beautiful to me, Rose, I've loved you since you first came here, darlin'. And don't you know Mrs. Hillyer loves you, too, that she's already taken you into her heart, and that that woman's heart is as wide as the blue sky itself and as deep as the stars?
Person2: Oh, I know. She's so sweet, she's been so good to me...
Person1: Do you know what a friend you have got there? Do you know she would fight for you like a tiger, that she would fly to your defense in an instant with all the courage in her soul if anyone tried to hurt you? Is this any way to repay her trust and love? Are you ashamed as I am ashamed?  Don't cry, honey, don't cry. But let me warn you, damn your hide, this is Thermopylae and I am standing here. Do you hear me, damn you. I am standing at Thermopylae and the Persians shall not pass! Now get your tail out of here and go wash those dishes, and stop crying!

Person1: I won't let her in. I'm sorry, Randy, I'm not gonna let her in. She - Well, I'm not gonna let her in, the way she's behaving. I'm - I'm -
Person2: Not gonna let me in?
Person1: Who knows what you're gonna do in there? I don't want you -
Person2: What do you mean, what am I gonna do? I'm in the same clothes for two days! I want to get a few of my things! Big deal!

Person1: Why did you hold his collar?
Person2: What?
Person1: What?  What?  That boy, that little boy, you were holding his collar, gripping his collar, what for?
Person2: Would you let me pass?
Person1: Is he next?  Do you drag him into your little room?  Where is it?  Is this it?
Person2: Don't do this.
Person1: I've watched you - on verandahs, at Garden Parties, at the Races - how can you stand there?  How can you ever smile?  As if your life hadn't capsized?
Person2: You know why? He tries to hold her. She resists
Person1: Dance with me.
Person2: No.
Person1: Dance with me.  I want to touch you. I want the things which are mine. Which belong to me.
Person2: Do you think you're the only one who feels anything?  Is that what you think?

Person1: Who paid you to see me off?
Person2: I can't Jack. How can I?
Person1: Yes you can.

Person1: Jefferson City?
Person2: No, Leavenworth.
Person1: I've never been in there. They just jumped you out of the blue?
Person2: I had to get up anyway.
Person1: Me, I'm riding along, minding my own business. Four cowboys come by and we decide to ride together for a while, friendly as can be. I always figure you might as well approach life like everybody's your friend or nobody is... don't make much difference. We get out in the middle of that frying pan and suddenly everybody's pointing their gun but me. I guess they admired my horse. She's a great one, a sweet little bay.
Person2: Looks like that's not all they admired.
Person1: Yup. The whole rig. I don't care much about the rest, but I surely will miss that bay. Least they didn't kill me. That was right considerate, I thought. They were laughing when they left me. Thought it was real funny. I walked for a little while but there was no use, so I gave it up. Figured it was just bad luck.

Person1: Something's wrong with you.
Person2: Nothing is wrong with me.  I know something's in there - and so do you.

Person1: What is the matter with you? You're trembling?
Person2: Am I? Why, I - I don't know any reason why I should be, unless of course it's you . . .
Person1: Me?
Person2: Being alone with you has always done this to me. You know that.
Person1: For a celebrated bounder, that is an awful admission. Besides, I never knew that any female could do this to you .
Person2: Well, you can. You always could.
Person1: Liar! You're just suffering from lack of sleep.

Person1: So this Z...he fancies himself an individual?
Person2: Yeah...I mean...well...I don't know, really, sir.
Person1: Well now you haven't fallen for this silly idea of individuality, have you?
Person2: Oh, no, sir!
Person1: Good.  You're a good soldier.
Person2: Thank you, sir.

Person1: Hello, Spooky.
Person2: I don't want to hurt you, Catwoman. Yet.  After tomorrow, you can do anything you want, but please, just stay out of sight for the next 24 hours. I won't stand by and watch my leader get all emotional over an animal like you. I warn you, don't tempt Captain God when he is angry. Let is complete our mission in peace.
Person1: Whatever you say...Sis.

Person1: Yes...
Person2: You want me to wear white for you? You must be madder than I thought...
Person1: But ELLEN, I told you I have stopped...
Person2: But for how long - what will set you off again? You'll be walking down the street and some girl will be wearing a white hat - and that will be it - back on the merry go round...

Person1: It's important for you to know who your father was, isn't it?
Person2: Yeah, it is.

Person1: I've got what I need for my report. There is... there is one thing that bothers me though.
Person2: What?
Person1: It's not really my place, but it's not easy for me to set aside the private detective part of me either. See, I know a little about missing persons.  When kids run, they almost always leave a note.  It's guilt. They want to say goodbye.
Person2: There wasn't one.  The police looked.
Person1: Do you think the police did a good job?
Person2: I don't know.  I think so.
Person1: It is possible... and I know this isn't something you want to hear. Your daughter may have tried to hide a note where she thought you would eventually find it, but where she knew your husband would never find it.  She might have wanted to tell you something...
Person2: No.  You don't have any reason to think that...
Person1: If the police focused their search in her room, her belongings, well that'd be only natural, but they may have been looking in the wrong place.

Person1: I sacrificed so much to make sure you were comfortable and happy. After everything I’ve done for you...
Person2: I know you've done a lot, Daniel, but love isn't about keeping score.
Person1: It's not about that. I just wish you'd acknowledge and appreciate it sometimes.
Person2: I do appreciate it, but a relationship is a two-way street. We both have our parts to play.

Person1: I'm going to the powder room.
Person2: Sure, baby -- go anywhere you want.  But I have to give you an escort.  Security reasons.

Person1: I'll go out with you, or I'll stay here, and do anything you'd like for me to do... if you'll just do one thing. If you'll tell me that you love me.
Person2: You can sing the song.
Person1: You know what, you are never satisfied.
Person2: That's right, hand.

Person1: Getting anywhere with those?
Person2: There's some sort of pattern here. It'll take a minute.
Person1: Have you looked outside?  Jellyfish. Everywhere.  I hate jellyfish.  Harry, what happened in the cafeteria?
Person2: Don't do this.  Don't psychoanalyze me.  I hate squid.  Period.  Just like you hate jellyfish.
Person1: You said something to me, before -- about how we were all going to die.
Person2: Did I?
Person1: You don't remember that?
Person2: I don't remember much.  It's like my memory is on the tip of my tongue -- but I can't taste any of it.  Funny, my senses are much keener though. Purer.  Hearing, seeing, smelling. Like, I can smell your sheets, Norman.  You tried to wash it out earlier, But I can still smell the urine.  Don't worry, I'm not going to tell the others.  It's normal, really, isn't it?  In a crisis.  The stress. The panic.

Person1: Whoever tossed this place wants you.  And I'm looking to stay outta the line of fire.
Person2: How's this about me?
Person1: They lifted my wallet to make it look like a robbery.  But there's a bug in my computer keyboard, a tap on the phone and a mike in the light fixture.
Person2: Like you said, maybe somebody's tracking one of your investigations.
Person1: Hardware's too sophisticated.  It's gotta be the government.  And why do I think they're looking for you?
Person2: You're crazy.
Person1: I'm you, I take that money and get outta town while you can.

Person1: ...twenty-six minutes to get your shit together.
Person2: Let me talk to her again.
Person1: No.
Person2: I want'to talk to her.
Person1: Forget about it.

Person1: And probably never again. It's nothing personal. It's just...I know a lot of pilots. Maybe I'm immune...
Person2: Don't worry, I'm a new strain. And I don't give up. Everything I've ever wanted I've had to work like hell for. Well, how about it?
Person1: How about what?
Person2: How about anything, anything you want to do.
Person1: Hard to argue with that, isn't it...
Person2: A date... Coffee... A drink...A walk in the park.
Person1: What about the plane?
Person2: What plane.
Person1: Most of them invite me to sit in the cockpit...play with the levers and things. MAVERICK Well, get used to it.
Person1: Used to what?
Person2: I'm different.
Person1: I'm starting to sense that now.

Person1: Now, if you'll excuse me, I really must...
Person2: Wait. Stop. You don't seem to understand. You're not really comprehending any of this. I killed him. I did it, Carnes. I'm Patrick Bateman. I chopped Owen's head off. I tortured dozens of girls. The whole message I left on your machine was true.
Person1: Excuse me. I really must he going.
Person2: No! Listen, don't you know who I am? I'm not Davis, I'm Patrick Bateman! I talk to you on the phone all the time! Don't you recognize me? You're my lawyer.

Person1: How much?
Person2: Oh, about five hundred.
Person1: Do you really think you can beat him?
Person2: Of course he thinks he can beat me, Bert. He wouldn't be playing me if he didn't. Right, Felson?
Person1: I didn't ask him can he beat you. I already know he can beat you. I asked him will he? With Eddie, that's two different things.

Person2: You promised to let me kiss you.

One day, I was joking her, and asking her whether she would take the water again, whether she had found another lover, and so forth.  She suddenly burst out into tears, and, after a while, said to me.
Person2: Roderick, you know well enough that I have never loved but you!  Was I ever so wretched that a kind word from you did not make me happy? Ever so angry, but the least offer of good-will on your part did not bring me to your side?  Did I not give a sufficient proof of my affection for you in bestowing one of the finest fortunes of England upon you?  Have I repined or rebuked you for the way you have wasted it? No, I loved you too much and too fondly; I have always loved you. From the first moment I saw you, I saw your bad qualities, and trembled at your violence; but I could not help loving you.  I married you, though I knew I was sealing my own fate in doing so, and in spite of reason and duty.  What sacrifice do you want from me? Person2: Charlie,. I've been trying to postpone this. But what's the point? It's over.  I don't want to hurt you. This is very hard for me. I'm doing this for both of us. I really want you to be happy.
Person1: I will be happy if I have you. I love you.
Person2: That won't make any difference. We just can't live together. And you had the nerve to drive up with that bimbo Janet.
Person1: What are you talking about? Who's Janet?
Person2: I just can't trust you anymore.
Person1: What about everything I said to you this afternoon...
Person2: That's just it. You can always get to me. There's this window in my heart and every time I leave it open, you climb in. Unless I close it now, nothing's ever going to be different!
Person1: But what has to be different?
Person2: Everything. I have a good head for business, I should be franchising the bakery. And I want you to give me your word that whatever happens, you'll go to college. And finish.
Person1: What! What about the group and my singing career? What about me?
Person2: I'm trying to save you years of frustration... waiting for a big break... no. Waiting for that big disappointment so you could blame it all on me.
Person1: You don't know zip! You think I'm going to end up selling appliances like my father? Chasing women around the store. I've got to give it a shot. Why are you trying to kill the two things that mean the most to me? Until yesterday you loved me and you loved us.  ) What the hell has changed? For two years I've done nothing but love you. I'll show you, I'm going to be just like Fabian!

Person1: Positive.
Person2: Did you do everything I asked you to?
Person1: Yes.
Person2: You told her I never said I love you before?
Person1: Yes!
Person2: You told her that people are jealous cause I'm loaded?
Person1: Yes!
Person2: And you think she bought it?
Person1: I'm pretty sure she did.
Person2: Pretty sure or sure sure?
Person1: She bought it.
Person2: I'll be in touch.

Person1: We didn't sign on for this shit. This shit is intense. I need to call my agent.
Person2: Just shut up and do what they say. They're gonna kill us if we don't, Ian.

Person1: Do you think I'm having a good time? Do you think I'm just teasing you? Do you think I know whether I want you or Wally?
Person2: So we should "wait and see." For how long?
Person1: I grew up with Wally. I began my adult life with him.
Person2: Fine. That's all there is to it then.
Person1: No! That's not all there is to it! I love you, too--I know I do.
Person2: Okay, okay--I know you do, too.
Person1: It's a good thing I didn't have that baby, isn't it?

Person1: They're not bad.
Person2: No, they're not.  I'd say your investment was paying dividends.
Person1: My what?
Person2: Back in '37, you heard Benny Goodman play for the first time, so you went out and got a used clarinet.  You wanted nothing more than to be able to play like him. You tried hard, but it wasn't long before it was clear that Benny Goodman would never be looking over his shoulder.  So you gave the clarinet to Spencer.
Person1: Huh.  That was nice of me.
Person2: You had a hidden agenda, though. See, when he was five or six, little Spence used to follow you around like a puppy.  Bothered the hell out of you.  But as soon as you gave him the clarinet...
Person1: ... he started practicing, and he left me alone from then on.
Person2: Exactly.  And he got good.
Person1: No kidding.

Person1: Please be a little more careful how you talk, Mr. Mitchell.
Person2: How careful should I be?
Person1: I don't like being referred to as babe, or addressed as baby. I don't like your possessive attitude, nor your hints of secret knowledge. In fact, to be very frank, Mr. Mitchell, I don't think I like you.
Person2: Maybe you're going to have to like me.

Person1: --Millie, you've got to stay out of the clubhouse.  It'll just get everybody in trouble.
Person2: I got lured.
Person1: You didn't get "lured".  Women never get lured.  They're too strong and powerful for that. Now say it--"I didn't get lured and I will take responsibility for my actions".
Person2: "I didn't get lured and I will take responsibility for my actions".
Person1: That's better.  Got the radar ready?

Person1: Well, I got up one morning and went to work... just like any other day, except it was my first since hearing about the baby. And, I... I felt this fear and anxiety washing over me.  I looked around, and I thought, how can we raise a child surrounded by all this?  How can a child grow up here?  So, that night, I told her I didn't want us to have it, and over the next few weeks, I convinced her it was wrong.  I mean... I wore her down, slowly.
Person2: I want to have children.  It's just...
Person1: I can tell you now, I know... I'm positive I made the right decision.  I'm positive. But, there's never a day that passes that I don't wish I had decided differently.

Person1: It's still there, Jake.  Even if you never say a word about it. You can't spend two years in Vietnam ...
Person2: What does that have to do with anything? Does it explain the barricaded subway stations? Does it explain those Godforsaken creatures?
Person1: New York is filled with creatures. Everywhere. And lots of stations are closed.
Person2: They're like demons, Jez.
Person1: Demons, Jake? Come on. They're winos and bag ladies. Low life. That's all they are. The streets are crawling with 'em. Don't make em into somethin' they're not.  It's the pressure, honey. That's all it is.
Person2: Those guys tried to kill me tonight. They were aiming right at me.
Person1: Kids on a joy ride. Happens all the time.
Person2: They weren't human!
Person1: Come on. What were they, Jake?

Person1: I'm saving them for a rainy day.
Person2: gives her a sympathetic look.
Person2: I know it's hard for you being in here, but it is for the good of your health.
Person1: They sent me off to the Bahamas "for the good of my health." They sent me to the Bay of bloody Islands "for the good of my health."
Person2: looks startled at the outburst.
Person1: I'm sorry, Mrs. Rieper. I'm feeling quite fatigued.
Person2: We don't want to tire you out, dear.
Person2: stands and picks up her handbag. Pauline stands and Juliet grabs her hand.
Person1: Can't you stay a bit longer, Paul?

Person1: I heard about the disagreement in the meeting. People can be so narrow-minded.
Person2: It's frustrating. I genuinely believe my approach is the right one.
Person1: Everyone thinks you’re wrong, but I’m on your side. I think your vision is exactly what the project needs.
Person2: That means a lot, Cassie. Sometimes I feel like I'm fighting this battle alone.
Person1: You're not alone. And together, I think we can persuade the others to see things our way.

Person1: Look what you made me do!
Person2: What's wrong with you?!

Person1: There's one thing I don't understand.  The thing I don't understand ... is every confusing thing you're saying.
Person2: Look, mush, you're the guv'nor here, I can see that, I'm on your manor now, right.  So there's no need to get out of your pram.  I'm Johnny-come-lately to all this.  Whatever the bollocks between you and this slag Valentine, it's got nothing to do with me.  I don't wanna know.
Person1: Well, I'll tell you.  I believe this Valentine screwed me out of a fair sum of money.
Person2: I can well believe it.  I'm sure he has done, son.  He's about as straight as a dog's hind leg.
Person1: But I can't be sure.  I don't even know who he is.  He's too insulated.  Too many layers around him.
Person2: Your guess is as good as mine, mate.  I'm here on another matter entirely.
Person1: Yeah, I guess you are.
Person2: Good job your lot showed up when they did or it would've been me for the high jump.
Person1: That dude who works for Valentine.  He's the one sent those guys after you.  You know that.
Person2: Yeah.  Shouldn't wonder.  Must've done.
Person1: So what's your beef, pal?
Person2: Nothing financial.  Strictly personal.  I can see how all this import-export malarkey might give rise to confusion where I'm concerned.  A foreigner, showing up unexpectedly, like.
Person1: It was you.  Downtown.
Person2: Eh?
Person1: Because that wasn't anything to do with me.  And suspicion has been cast in my direction.  Didn't make any sense.  Choosing those shitheads over me, cutting me out of the deal, then screwing them over too.
Person2: No, I can reassure you on that point. Valentine was just as surprised by that turn of events as you.
Person1: He'd already grabbed more than his allotted cut.  Didn't think he'd be so bold as to take all of it.
Person2: All of what?
Person1: Of the deal, man.
Person2: Oh, yeah, right.  The deal.
Person1: But if you're mad at him too and he's mad at you ... that must make us pals.
Person2: As you prefer, squire.  As you prefer.
Person1: In which case I'll just do what I usually do.
Person2: And that is?
Person1: What am I doing?

Person1: Rose.
Person2: Kitty...
Person1: Rose, we have a crisis.  I am sure that you are aware of the horrible allegations against Jim Cunningham.
Person2: Yes, I saw the news. Something about a kiddie-porn dungeon.
Person1: Please! Don't say those words.  Well... as you can see... many of us are devastated by this news. This is obviously some kind of conspiracy meant to destroy an innocent man. And I have taken it upon myself to spearhead the Jim Cunningham defence campaign. But unfortunately my civic duties have created a conflict of interest... which involves you.
Person2: Beg pardon?
Person1: Rose... I have to appear at his arraignment tomorrow morning. And as you know, the girls also leave for Los Angeles tomorrow morning. Now, as their coach... I was the obvious choice to chaperone them on the trip.
Person2: But now you can't go.
Person1: Yes. And believe me, of all the other mothers I would never dream of asking you, given the predicament with your son. But none of the other mothers are able to go.
Person2: Oh, Kitty, I don't know. This is so last-minute... Eddie is in New York...
Person1: Rose... I don't know if you realise how great an opportunity this is for our daughters. This has been a dream of ours for a long time.  Sometimes I doubt your commitment to Sparkle Motion.

Person1: I'm sorry, darlin', I hate it as much as you do.
Person2: Well, she does seem to cause a lot of trouble, I admit. But she doesn't mean any harm and it'll break the children's hearts, they all love her.
Person1: It's because of the children she's got to go. I don't want to be holier-than-thou, but the girl doesn't have the same outlook we do. Morals don't mean a thing to her, not a thing, and we can't have a girl like that in this house.
Person2: Who is to say our morals are better than hers?  But maybe you're right. She could... influence Dolly...
Person1: That is exactly what I'm afraid of.  The girl has got to go, darlin'.
Person2: But morals come from the heart, not from rigid rules and empty laws. And in her heart Rose has never hurt anyone and she never would. Therefore, she's not immoral. Not really.
Person1: Now look, darlin', this is no time to go off into the fourth dimension.
Person2: But what I said is the simple truth. And now I see something else, I see more deeply into it...

Person1: Pay money?
Person2: Yeah, dummy.  Money.
Person1: I never paid for it in my life.
Person2: It's better when you pay... they let you do anything.
Person1: They always let me do anything. I don't want to pay for it.  I never paid for it in my life.
Person2: Just do what I say, okay? We'll pay for the girls and have a good time... Don't you trust me?

Person1: I keep hearing you helped a lot of Jews to escape. How did you do that?
Person2: I don't remember in detail, there were so many.
Person1: Did you call someone you knew?
Person2: I may have, as... as I said, I simply don't remember.
Person1: Let me me help you, then. You picked up the phone and made a call -  'Hello, Adolf? Wilhelm speaking. Listen, old pal, there's a Jew-boy musician I want you to help. He needs a permit to get to Paris.'

Person1: Bernie.
Person2: Well, obviously it's me, isn't it -- I work in the City in a job I don't understand and everyone keeps getting promoted above me.  I haven't had a girlfriends since... puberty and, well, the long and short of it is, nobody fancies me, and if these cheeks get any chubbier, they never will.

Person1: I can't wait to tell Reverend Mother about all my experiences.  I've learned so much.
Person2: Well, you know, you don't have to tell her everything.  I mean, nothing really happened.  We just... took a walk.

Person1: Hey, guys. How's it going?
Person2: Excellent. My friend and I are up here doing the wine tour, and he tells me that you folks make one hell of a Syrah.
Person1: That's what people say.

Person1: Owwww...You're hurting me... You're hurting me...I hate you ...I hate you...
Person2: You're no bargain either, pal.
Person1: I want my mommy...I want my mommy..

Person1: Brian. What's wrong?
Person2: You told Swayzak about our arson lead. It's all over the news.
Person1: I didn't know it was a secret. There aren't supposed to be secrets between the city and its investigators --
Person2: -- Bullshit! You knew what I told you wasn't ready for the papers --
Person1: Will you please keep your voice down, there's people --
Person2: -- You could have scared the son of a bitch off. We may never bust him now. All for a couple's political points.
Person1: I was doing my job.
Person2: Yeah? And just how much of all this has been "doing your job"?
Person1: Let me ask you something, do you really think Marty had you assigned to arson because of your firefighting skills? Who the hell are you kidding? I was there, remember? I saw you and your brother --
Person2: Leave Stephen out of this --
Person1: Oh yeah, he's the real fireman.  Who are you? Just another probie working for Swayzak --
Person2: -- I work for the city.
Person1: You knew what we were asking you to do. Don't suddenly pull out a conscience now. The fit isn't right.

Person1: It belongs to me, Miz Lampert -- an' if you don't give it to me your life ain't gonna be worth the paper it's printed on. You savvy what I'm sayin', Miz Lampert?
Person2: Please stop -- please!
Person1: You think on it real careful-like, Miz Lampert -- y'hear?

Person1: You see, Karl, growing up, only knew that sex was wrong and that people who did it should be killed for it. He couldn't really read but, well, neither could his mother. But, his father made sure that his mother knew what the Bible said. And she made sure Karl knew. You know he slept in a hole in the ground under a toolshed, right?
Person2: I knew he slept in a toolshed.
Person1: His mother told him that he was their punishment. Hers and his father's; from God, for having sex--
Person2: Before they were married?
Person1: I don't think so. Just period, I think. She told him... God gave them the ugliest creation he could think of. Karl has an entire book -- a notebook. On every page it says "Franklin Chapter 1 Verse number 1." He wrote that a few years ago after he'd learned to write. His father's name was Franklin.
Person2: That's really strange. What does it mean?
Person1: One of his Daddy's Bible lessons I imagine. Y'all pull up a chair. I'll go out and talk to him.

Person1: All right, how about Ringo? I mean ... he's very upset, you know ... and as far as your girlfriend, little Audrey's concerned, she's finished with men for the rest of her natural, and another thing ...
Person2: A harmless bit of fun, aah, none of you have any sense of humour left these days.
Person1: Oh, it's all right for you but those two girls were scared to death! Honest, Grandad, why? I mean, why do you do these things?
Person2: You're left-handed, aren't you, Paul?
Person1: Yeah ... so what?
Person2: Why do you always use your left hand?
Person1: Well, don't be daft, I've got to.
Person2: And I take a left-handed view of life, I've got to.

Person1: When we talked earlier, it sounded like you weren't supportive of my decision to switch jobs.
Person2: You misunderstood what I said. I just want what's best for you.
Person1: I felt belittled by your comments, though.
Person2: Maybe you should listen more carefully next time.
Person1: It's not about listening, it's about how you said it.
Person2: You always take things too personally.

Person1: D'Artagnan, you will hunt down Aramis, Porthos and Athos, and bring me their heads, or I will have yours.  And as for you, Phillippe... Back to the prison you shall go, and into the mask you hate.
Person2: No, Louis!  No!
Person1: Wear it until you love it.  And die in it!!

Person1: That's five hundred dollars.
Person2: What?
Person1: Five hundred.
Person2: You're crazy -- it should be like two dollars!
Person1: I was wearing that dress the day I lost my virginity.
Person2: Well why do I care about that?
Person1: Why do you even want it? It would look stupid on you.
Person2: God, forget you!

Person1: There, okay? Now that's just plain old hamburger.
Person2: Some of the brown stuff is still there.
Person1: Then eat around it.
Person2: No.
Person1: Now listen to me, young man. Do you know what I had to go through to put this goddamn food on the goddamn table?
Person2: I don't care.  I hate it.  I want pizza.
Person1: Not on your life.  That's it. I've had it with crap around this house.  From now on, no more pizza!  Get it?  Starting right now you can eat real food like a normal human being!
Person2: No!

text:
Person1: That man's a black angel, Sailor. You hook up with him, you'll regret it. If you live to.
Person2: Thanks, darlin', I know you got my best interest in mind, and I 'preciate it sincerely. I love you, but I gotta sleep now.

Person1: Youre right. I was so desperate to make everyone believe I was happy that I spent six grand on a lie. And after all that? The only one who ended up falling for it was me.
Person2: Go ahead, Kat. Hate me. You were running out of steam on the whole Jeffrey thing. Maybe now youll be able to hold onto this just long enough to ruin your next relationship.

Person1: I've known soldiers who've had their heads blown off who were more intelligent than you two.  Not only have you failed to protect your most valuable possession from theft - but you didn't even know it'd been stolen!  I'd sooner buy heroin from the guy who sells drugs outside my grandson's school than anything from you guys.
Person2: I am sorry you feel that way.
Person1: And I'm sorry you look that way, short-ass.  That suit stinks and you obviously dye your hair.

Person1: Oh, I get it... I guess you think you're too good for the simple pleasures of Halloween.
Person2: No, just a little too old.
Person1: Oh really, well I hope something changes your mind some day.

Person1: Do you really believe your life is meaningless?
Person2: I don't know.  But as a scientist I have to consider that possibility.
Person1: And yet you're willing to die for this cause, the one thing that's given your life a sense of purpose. Don't you see the contradiction here -- ?
Person2: It's getting late...
Person1: What are you so afraid of, Ellie?

Person1: Guess I made a boo-boo, huh?
Person2: No -- I like it.
Person1: Really? You mean you wouldn't be ashamed to be seen with somebody in a hat like this?
Person2: Of course not.
Person1: Maybe if I wore it a little more to the side --  is that better?
Person2: Much better.
Person1: Well, as long as you wouldn't be ashamed to be seen with me -- how about the three of us going out this evening -- you and me and the bowler -- stroll down Fifth Avenue -- sort of break it in --
Person2: This is a bad day for me.
Person1: I understand. Christmas -- family and all that --
Person2: I'd better get back to my elevator. I don't want to be fired.
Person1: Oh, you don't have to worry about that. I have quite a bit of influence in Personnel. You know Mr. Sheldrake?
Person2: Why?
Person1: He and I are like this.  Sent me a Christmas card. See?

Person1: Mrs. Lampert --
Person2: What do you want?
Person1: Didn't Charles tell you, Mrs. Lampert?
Person2: Tell me what?
Person1: It doesn't belong to you, Mrs. Lampert -- you do know that, don't you?
Person2: I don't know anything.
Person1: Mrs. Lampert, any morning now you could wake up dead.
Person2: Leave me alone -- !
Person1: Dead, Mrs. Lampert -- like last week's news -- like Charles, Mrs. Lampert --
Person2: Stop it!

Person1: I'll go first.
Person2: No -- I will.
Person1: I stood up first.
Person2: That doesn't matter.
Person1: You always get to go first.

Person1: Mr. Rasczak.
Person2: Rasczak... Silly name. There ought to be a law against using a school as a recruiting station !
Person1: No, Mr. Rasczak doesn't do that at all. He sorta discourages you really.
Person2: Good, because you're going to Harvard, and that's the end of it.
Person1: I'm eighteen. It's my decision.
Person2: Oh, is that how it is ?

Person1: Come on, you! Before we slap you down.
Person2: Do you want us to call the cops and have them give you the boots?
Person1: Where is he, before we beat it out of you?

Person1: Feeling better, Oz?
Person2: I'm such a loser.
Person1: That's the spirit.

Person1: What are you going to do?
Person2: In the road to survival, there is always sacrifice Logan.  We are the future... not them.  We must protect ourselves.  After tonight, when the leaders of the world return home -- They will return as brothers, as mutants... and our cause will be theirs.  This is survival, I know you understand that, Logan... or is it Wolverine?

Person1: I ain't never used no hatchet that I remember.
Person2: You're just crazy in a retarded kind of way then.  It wouldn't matter to me if you did do violence on somebody 'cause I ain't afraid of shit. You think I'm afraid for you to stay here. You're just a bit odd it looks like to me. Not really, I'm just jokin' with you. Welcome to our humble home, buddy. Frank needs all the friends he can get. Frank's a real weak little kid. His daddy taught him how to be a timid person.

Person1: Yeah?
Person2: Mr. Malkovich?
Person1: Who's calling?
Person2: You don't know me, but I'm a great admirer of yours.
Person1: How'd you get this number?
Person2: It's just that I fantasize about you and, well, speaking to you now has gotten me sort of excited and...

Person1: ...You don't?
Person2: Nah, that's all forgotten.
Person1: ...It is?
Person2: Sure, Mr. Muss -- uh, Sid said I could have the job back.
Person1: Absolutely, Buzz, I'm glad he --
Person2: But he told me you stole that swell hoop idea from me. What gives!
Person1: But, Buzz --
Person2: Say, that was a swell idea!
Person1: But, Buzz, you know I never --
Person2: And Sid says you stole it!
Person1: But Buzz --

Person1: Just for a second. Please, Rose, what's the harm?
Person2: Buddy, you don't realize it but what you're doing isn't nice.
Person1: Aw, come on, Rose, I want to see what the nipple on it is like.
Person2: You don' t need to know that. It's none of your business.
Person1: Aw, be a good sport, Rose.  You like me, don't you? I like you a lot.

Person1: Have you forgotten about your sister?
Person2: They're gonna kill us. They get us across the border, they're gonna take us out in the desert and shoot us.
Person1: If they get over the border, they're gonna let us go.
Person2: Dad, I watch those reality shows. They never let anybody go. Any cop will tell you, in a situation like this, you get a chance, you go for it. This is our chance.
Person1: What about Kate?
Person2: They're gonna kill her anyway. At least now with all these cops we've got a fighting chance.
Person1: Son, I have this situation under control. I know exactly what I'm doing. You're going to have to trust me on this.
Person2: If trusting you means trusting those killers, I can't do that. If you don't tell the cops, I will.

Person1: Let's have sex.  I thought you were curious.
Person2: Not about that.
Person1: I'm listening.
Person2: I'm afraid to tell you.
Person1: I'm not armed.  Well?
Person2: Why is it wrong to run?
Person1: You shouldn't even think such things...  And you picked a strange person to say them to --
Person2: I suppose. But what if you want to live?
Person1: So? Do what everyone does. Try like hell for renewal.

Person1: Fine. All of you want to be angry? Be angry... but I'm the one who took the ball and that makes it mine.
Person2: No.
Person1: Give it to me, Sami.
Person2: No!

Person1: My little French.
Person2: Stop. Get away.
Person1: What is it?
Person2: You have to choose Nathan. It's like Sophie's choice. Only it is Nathan's choice. Did you ever see that movie, Sophie's Choice? It is like that. Only it is this.
Person1: Gabby, you know I'm trying to sort things out.
Person2: No! It is now that you must decide.  I love you, Doctor Nathan...  ...but I will not wait. I will not be your chippy. I will not be your little Mademoiselle Parlez-vous side dish.  My love. I want to have a sweet tiny baby inside my belly... from you.

Person1: You want the tapes for yourself.     You're going to sell them.
Person2: I'm going to retire with a shit-load of money. Find me a small country that doesn't have an extradition treaty with the States and live the good life.
Person1: You didn't have to kill Sophie.
Person2: Yes I did.    Lose ends are messy.
Person1: What about me? Aren't I a loose end?
Person2: When this is over you can say whatever the hell you want. I'll be long gone. Besides, who's going to believe you? You're just a two-bit crook.
Person1: And you're a dirty cop. At least I don't pretend to be something different than what I am.

Person1: You're perplexing to me these days, Wendell.  You're not your old, cruel self anymore.  I need proof that the extracurricular work I had planned for you remains within your grasp.
Person2: What work?
Person1: I've long been involved in containing hard crime in such a way that myself and a few colleagues might someday enjoy a profit dispensation.  That day will soon be here and you'll share handsomely.  Grand means will be in our hands, Wendell.
Person1: Imagine crime limited to the criminal element who perpetrate it. Imagine the means to keep the nigger filth sedated.  But don't stop there.  Extrapolate.  Imagine the police in control.  It's big, lad.
Person2: You lost me, Dudley.  I don't know what you're talking about.
Person1: You have your extracurricular secrets, I have mine.  We'll hold a clarification session soon.  For now, I need your fearsome old habits at the Victory Motel. We're going to brace a man who may know who killed Jack Vincennes. Can I count on you?
Person2: Sure, boss.  Sure you can.

Person1: That's not technically accurate - SIMPSONS murders weren't sexually motivated.
Person2: You know that circulation is down for three months in a row.
Person1: I heard something like that
Person2: Then it's going to be 'I killed for sex'... Listen darling, why won't you let us use your real name for the features - if people knew that it was the ELLEN CARTER writing the features and not just some pseudonym - then we might shift more units. You haven't written anything in four years darling - I know you need this job - and if circulation keeps dropping, there's not going to be a magazine for you to write for...

Person1: Take the radio, stay down here in the lobby, watch the elevator.
Person2: What?  Daniel, I wanna go upstairs.

Person1: Lawyers.  I don't know, Ellie -- Firing me is going to be very tough on you.  It's going to be hard to get over.  There will be therapy bills for you.

Person1: Well, if the Clock People give you any inside information on the end of the world, drop us a postcard.
Person2: The world isn't going to end, you dummy; I hope you know that much.  But it is going to change. It's going to change drastically, and probably in your lifetime. The Clock People see calamitous earthquakes as the agent of change, and they may be right, since there are a hundred thousand earthquakes a year and major ones are long overdue. But there are far worse catastrophes coming... unless the human race can bring itself to abandon the goals and values of civilization, in other words, unless it can break the consumption habit -- and we are so conditioned to consuming as a way of life that for most of us life would have no meaning without the yearnings and rewards of progressive consumption. It isn't merely that our bad habits will cause global catastrophes, but that our operative political-economic philosophies have us in such a blind crab grip that they prevent us from preparing for the natural disasters that are not our fault. So the apocalyptic shit is going to hit the fan, all right, but there'll be some of us it'll miss. Little pockets of humanity. Like the Clock People. Like you two honeys, if you decide to accept my offer of a lease on Siwash Cave. There's almost no worldwide calamity -- famine, nuclear accident, plague, weather warfare or reduction of the ozone shield -- that you couldn't survive in that cave.
